summary: Joel Spolsky recounts a FogBugz demo event in Chicago where the choice of a cheap hotel — the Congress Plaza — backfired badly. The hotel was shabby, the staff was on strike forcing attendees to cross a picket line, and the depressing environment likely hurt the perception of the product being presented. He reflects on how the physical environment of a business presentation psychologically colors the audience's impression of the product itself. He also mentions speaking at the Inc. 500 Conference about Fog Creek's hiring practices and promotes his upcoming FogBugz 6.0 launch in New York.
key_points: Choosing a cheap hotel for a business demo created a negative atmosphere that likely hurt the product's perception among attendees
The physical environment of a presentation psychologically influences how the audience perceives the product being presented
Hotel quality indicators can be counterintuitive — shabby hotels sometimes have plasma TVs as a facade of modernity while genuinely nice hotels stick with older equipment
The Congress Plaza Hotel staff was on strike, forcing demo attendees to cross a picket line — a situation Joel hadn't thought to check for when booking
Joel spoke at the Inc. 500 Conference about Fog Creek's hiring process, linking to his well-known series on finding and interviewing developers
money_quotes: Psychologically, I think that people tend to associate the environment they're in with the presentation.
When a demo is in a modern, new, shiny business hotel, it's like a little one hour vacation in luxuryland.

But when you go to a demo at the Congress Plaza and the rug is stained and there are fluorescent lights everywhere and the bathroom looks like LaGuardia airport, some of that general depressing aura of shabbiness will rub off on the product being presented.
I guess shabby hotels just treat their employees shabbily.
The nice chains, inexplicably, have old fashioned big-ass tube TVs. The ancient rotting edifices have 32" plasmas.
The usual nice words you might use to describe such a hotel would be 'threadbare' or 'shabby.' Other words ('maccabre,' 'Barton Fink,' and 'scuzzy') come to mind.
key_insight: The physical environment where you present your product psychologically shapes how the audience perceives that product, so cutting corners on venue quality is a false economy.
